parents.father_research_notes: Simeon Daggett (1758-1819). Per WikiTree (cited in Sam's research). Likely descended from the Massachusetts colonial Daggett line (John Daggett of Watertown MA, immigrant ~1630s).
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Dean (1758-c.1819). The matching birth/death dates with Simeon (per WikiTree) are likely a data error in the WikiTree record — almost certainly Anna had different death date than Simeon. Maiden name Dean.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Clay Hooker, Arizona cattle baron
narrative_blocks.body: She raised eleven children at Hinsdale on the Connecticut River. Her son Henry Clay Hooker founded the Sierra Bonita Ranch in Arizona Territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Daggetts married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1816 marriage to Henry Hooker came four years after Elisabeth Daggett, in a separate Hooker branch, married Edward Hooker. The two Daggett brides may share earlier Massachusetts ancestry.

person.siblings_research: Per WikiTree: 8 documented Daggett siblings: Simeon Jr (M, 1780-1854), Sarah M. (F, 1783-?), (unnamed son b.1784-?), Nathaniel (M, 1785-1826), Nancy (F, 1788-1854), Caleb C (M, 1792-1879), Silvey (F, 1792-1883 — possibly twin of Caleb), and Mary herself (1795-1864). Mary was the youngest of 8.

parents.father_research_notes: Simeon Daggett (1758-1819). Per WikiTree (cited in Sam's research). Likely descended from the Massachusetts colonial Daggett line (John Daggett of Watertown MA, immigrant ~1630s).
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Dean (1758-c.1819). The matching birth/death dates with Simeon (per WikiTree) are likely a data error in the WikiTree record — almost certainly Anna had different death date than Simeon. Maiden name Dean.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Clay Hooker, Arizona cattle baron
narrative_blocks.body: She raised eleven children at Hinsdale on the Connecticut River. Her son Henry Clay Hooker founded the Sierra Bonita Ranch in Arizona Territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Daggetts married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1816 marriage to Henry Hooker came four years after Elisabeth Daggett, in a separate Hooker branch, married Edward Hooker. The two Daggett brides may share earlier Massachusetts ancestry.

X02247.siblings_research: Per WikiTree: 8 documented Daggett siblings: Simeon Jr (M, 1780-1854), Sarah M. (F, 1783-?), (unnamed son b.1784-?), Nathaniel (M, 1785-1826), Nancy (F, 1788-1854), Caleb C (M, 1792-1879), Silvey (F, 1792-1883 — possibly twin of Caleb), and Mary herself (1795-1864). Mary was the youngest of 8.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Inherited the Hinsdale homestead from his father Seth; died in the house where he had been born ninety-three years earlier.

career.notes: Learned the trade in early life; pursued carriage making while also farming the homestead.
Worked the homestead through old age; gave full attention to farming after abandoning the carriage trade.

narrative_blocks.header: His sons launched three California empires
narrative_blocks.body: Six of his eleven children left Hinsdale for the West. Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch, and John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson.

narrative_blocks.header: Ninety-three years in one Hinsdale farmhouse
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 1791 in the room overlooking the Gay homestead and dying in March 1885, he passed his entire life on one farm in Hinsdale. The Gazetteer of Cheshire County called him 'a hale and hearty old man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed to California twice in his eighties
narrative_blocks.body: After eighty he made two trips out to visit his California sons, riding the railroads across a continent he had been born too early to see imagined. He returned each time to the Hinsdale homestead.

narrative_blocks.header: Amelia died young
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia died in 1817, in her first year.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born and raised on the homestead; married Henry M. Jones at Hinsdale in 1842 and remained in town.

parents.father_research_notes: Died when Henry was about two years old.
parents.mother_research_notes: Removed family to Athol, Massachusetts after husband's death.

career.notes: About four years in Hinds's household, starting around age fourteen.
Bought a saw-mill on Kilbourn brook a short distance from the village, ran it about three years.
Manufacturer of doors, sash, blinds, and dressed lumber for many years on Canal Street.
Served three years as selectman; re-elected to a fourth term but resigned.
Representative for Hinsdale 1876–77; also delegate to the 1876 New Hampshire Constitutional Convention.

narrative_blocks.header: Orphaned at two, brought to Hinsdale at twelve
narrative_blocks.body: His father Cyrus Jones died when he was about two years old and his mother Sally Bowker moved the family to Athol, Massachusetts. At twelve he was sent to Hinsdale to live with his uncle Windsor Bowker; he never left.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Julia Worthington Hooker, December 1842
narrative_blocks.body: He married Julia W., daughter of Henry Hooker of the Hinsdale homestead, on 7 December 1842. They raised three children, two of whom became mechanics in Keene.

narrative_blocks.header: Built a Canal Street doors-and-sash factory
narrative_blocks.body: After three years running a Kilbourn brook sawmill in the mid-1830s, he bought a shop on Canal Street in Hinsdale village and manufactured doors, sash, blinds, and dressed lumber for many years. The shop was a fixture of the manufacturing village.

narrative_blocks.header: Constitutional convention delegate, 1876
narrative_blocks.body: He served three terms as selectman, represented Hinsdale in the state legislature in 1876–77, and sat as a delegate to the 1876 New Hampshire constitutional convention. Earlier he had been captain of the Ashuelot Guards, a Hinsdale militia company.

narrative_blocks.header: Born in Gerry, Massachusetts — renamed Phillipston, 1814
narrative_blocks.body: His birthplace was named for Elbridge Gerry, fifth vice-president of the United States, but the town was renamed Phillipston in 1814 after Gerry's political fall from grace. He was thus born in Gerry but always identified as a Phillipston native.

research_sources.page: Hinsdale chapter — Henry M. Jones biographical sketch

burial.plot_notes: Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA — same cemetery as his uncle Henry Clay Hooker (H02705). No CEM entry yet.

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle and nephew both buried at LA's Angelus-Rosedale

narrative_blocks.header: Followed his uncle's path to Los Angeles burial
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Hinsdale in 1866 to Henry M. Jones and Julia Worthington Hooker, he migrated to southern California and died in Los Angeles in 1933. He was buried at Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery — the same cemetery where his uncle Henry Clay Hooker, the Arizona cattle king, had been buried twenty-six years earlier in 1907.

narrative_blocks.header: Hinsdale farmboy who ended in Pasadena
narrative_blocks.body: His parents were forty-eight and fifty-five when he was born — a late child of the Jones-Hooker marriage. The 1930 census found him in Pasadena, far from the Connecticut River Valley where his Hooker grandparents had farmed for four generations.

research_sources.page: Hinsdale chapter — George P. Hooker sketch

cross_connections.display_label: Followed uncle Henry Clay to Arizona cattle ranching

narrative_blocks.header: Followed his uncle Henry Clay to Arizona ranching
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Henry Clay Hooker had founded the Sierra Bonita Ranch in 1872 — Arizona's first American cattle ranch. By 1885 the Gazetteer of Cheshire County recorded that Cassius 'owned a cattle ranch in Arizona,' making him the second-generation Hooker rancher in the territory.

research_sources.page: Hinsdale chapter — George P. Hooker sketch

narrative_blocks.header: George died young
narrative_blocks.body: George died in 1858 at the age of one.

sources.short: San Francisco city directories 1866-1905 (Hooker & Co. partner)
Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hooker brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay arrived in San Francisco in spring 1851 and partnered with younger brother John Delos Hooker at Hooker & Co., 117–119 California Street — the hardware fortune that later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson. Older brother Henry Clay was simultaneously building Arizona's largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the Sons of the American Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Gay Hooker joined the Sons of the American Revolution, formally documenting his descent from a Revolutionary-era family. He died of pneumonia at age 83 on November 19, 1905, at his home at 967 Bush Street in San Francisco. Buried at Mountain View Cemetery in Oakland — the Bay Area's premier Victorian garden cemetery. (A Findagrave geocoding error lists the death location as 'San Francisco del Mar Municipality, Oaxaca, Mexico'; the actual location is San Francisco, California.)

narrative_blocks.header: A homestead chair followed him to San Francisco
narrative_blocks.body: He took a child's side chair from the Hinsdale homestead with him to San Francisco in 1851. The chair returned east after his 1905 death and now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society in Concord, provenanced back to his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay.

cross_connections.display_label: Carried his great-grandfather Bunker's Gay surname as a middle name

burial.plot_notes: Mountain View Cemetery, Oakland, Alameda County CA (per Findagrave Memorial 255641260).

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother John Delos Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Came to San Francisco in spring 1851; built fortune in hardware import. Hooker & Co. imported hardware, iron, and related goods. Partnered with brother John Delos Hooker (H02709, who later founded the Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory).

Per Truman & Hooker partner biography (re. son Osgood): 'Charles G. Hooker, a prominent hardware merchant of California, who came to this State in 1852, and still resides in San Francisco.' Charles arrived CA 1852 — placed him in the second wave of Gold Rush migrants (1849-1852). Hardware merchandise business connects to the broader CA development boom serving miners + settlers.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing to California in 1851; the family chair he took with him now sits at the New Hampshire Historical Society.

bio.extra_names: Tony Hooker pedigree records full first name as 'Arrabella' — formal name 'Arrabella Maria Celia Osgood'. EH 1909 lists her simply as 'Maria Celia (Osgood)' (using middle/Latin name in everyday use).

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma, San Mateo County CA. Same as wife Ella Wall Goad. (per Findagrave)

careers.employer: First National Bank of San Francisco
careers.notes: Per period business biography: 'He was receiving teller in the First National Bank from 1881 till 1885' — entry-level banking position from age 21 to 25.

careers.employer: Truman & Hooker (hay presses, agricultural implements, wagons, buggies)
careers.notes: Joined Irwin J. Truman's existing firm in 1885 at age 25, leaving his First National Bank position. The firm had been founded by Truman in 1867 at 1717 Front Street SF as a manufacturer and dealer in HAY PRESSES; in ~1882 (after 15 years) they added agricultural implements + wagons + buggies. Osgood had charge of the buying and selling departments. Wholesale + retail; trade extended over the Pacific Coast and to the Sandwich Islands [Hawaii], with goods also shipped to Russia and Egypt. ~30 employees. Per period business biography: 'Mr. Hooker is a quick, intelligent and obliging business man, a Californian of whom California has no reason to be ashamed.'

education.notes: Per period business biography: 'educated in the public schools of this city [SF] and at Exeter, New Hampshire.' Phillips Exeter Academy is the prestigious New England preparatory school. The NH connection ties to his father's New Hampshire roots (Charles Gay Hooker b. Hinsdale NH 1820).

memberships.notes: Listed in 1905 Officers & Members directory; address 16 Drumm
Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'HOOKER, G. Osgood' — alongside brother Robert G. Hooker and brother-in-law George H. Lent.

One of SF's most exclusive private clubs. Membership inferred from Sam's earlier note about Robert G. Hooker + Eugene Lent being members; Osgood likely also a member as the elder brother. Specific roster year not yet confirmed.

narrative_blocks.header: Banker at twenty, importer at twenty-five
narrative_blocks.body: From 1881 to 1885 he was receiving teller at the First National Gold Bank of San Francisco. Then he joined Irwin Truman to form Truman and Hooker, importers of agricultural machinery.

narrative_blocks.header: Sold San Francisco wagons to Russia and Egypt
narrative_blocks.body: Truman and Hooker's trade extended across the Pacific to Hawaii, then on to Russia and Egypt. The reach was unusual for an 1890s San Francisco firm.

narrative_blocks.header: Phillips Exeter prepared him from California
narrative_blocks.body: He left San Francisco public schools for the New Hampshire boarding school in his teens. Eighty years later Jay Rockefeller would follow the same path.

narrative_blocks.header: His Bohemian Club fellow married his sister
narrative_blocks.body: In 1892 he and his brother Robert appeared on the Bohemian Club roster alongside George H. Lent. That same year Lent married their sister Bessie Augusta Hooker — bringing the brother-in-law into the family.

cross_connections.display_label: Bohemian Club fellows the year Lent married his sister Bessie.

military_service.notes: Per Findagrave: VVETERAN designation. Era and conflict unspecified — given his birth year 1860, he would have been ~38 at the Spanish-American War (1898) which is the most likely fit. Other possibilities: military service in California state militia. INVESTIGATION FLAG.

death.note: Per Tony Hooker pedigree + Sam's data: lived Hillsborough San Mateo County ~10 years before death; lived San Mateo CA in 1939

burial.plot_notes: Cremated at Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma but NOT INTERRED there — disposition of cremains uncertain. Per Findagrave: 'Per Cypress Lawn Records, Robert Hooker was cremated at their facility but not interred there. You can call the office at 650-755-0580 to inquire of the disposition of cremains. Perhaps these children of Charles are in the family Mausoleum???'

sources.short: Tony Hooker pedigree (descendant chart for Thomas Hooker)

careers.role: Real estate agent / house broker / rent collector / insurance agent
careers.employer: Hooker & Lent (Robert G. Hooker + George H. Lent)
careers.location: 14 Post Street, San Francisco CA (telephone: Private Exchange 120)
careers.notes: Real estate agency, house brokerage, rent collection, and insurance.

Real estate and investment firm. Founded with brother-in-law George Heydenfeldt Lent (X02298, husband of his sister Bessie H04268) and brother-in-law Eugene Lent. The firm operated through at least 1932 per SF Real Estate Board Yearbook; building referenced in 1916 SF Commercial Club records. Robert G. Hooker was a Pacific-Union Club member (1932 roster) — same as his Lent business partners.
Per 1880s SF directory: 'Hooker Robert G., cashier Am. Sugar Refinery Co., r. 917 Bush' — early-career position before founding Hooker & Lent.

memberships.notes: One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Robert was a member alongside his Lent business partners (his sister Bessie's husband George Heydenfeldt Lent and brother-in-law Eugene Lent).
One of the most exclusive private clubs in the western US. Per SFgenealogy 1932 roster.
Per Bohemian Club Officers & Members 1892 roster: 'HOOKER, Robert G.' — alongside brother G. Osgood Hooker and brother-in-law George H. Lent (married Bessie that same year).

Per Burlingame Country Club roster: 'HOOKER. ROBERT G.. Cunard Bldg.. 503 Market St.. San Francisco. Calif.' — listed alongside several other Hooker family members including 'Lent Hooker' (likely Rodman Lent Hooker HD3246), 'John R. Hooker' (likely John Rodman Hooker HD3243 at Curran & Dwyer), 'Osgood Hooker' (likely Osgood Hooker H05205 — C. Osgood's son), 'Mrs. C. Osgood Hooker' (Ella Wall Goodfellow Hooker, his sister-in-law widow), and 'Robert G. Hooker Jr.' (660 Market Street, Gladding McBean & Co).

narrative_blocks.header: Real estate broker with two brothers-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: He partnered with George and Eugene Lent in the Hooker & Lent firm at 503 Market Street in San Francisco. The firm operated through 1932 and built a long-running family-business network.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied mechanics at Berkeley, ended in real estate
narrative_blocks.body: He took a Ph.B. in Mechanics at the University of California, Berkeley. After early years as cashier at the American Sugar Refining Company, he settled into real estate brokerage.

narrative_blocks.header: Member of San Francisco's three elite clubs
narrative_blocks.body: He belonged to the Bohemian Club from 1892, the Pacific-Union Club, and the Burlingame Country Club. The Hooker family appeared on the Burlingame roster across three generations.

cross_connections.display_label: Brothers-in-law and partners in Hooker & Lent real estate.
Three brothers-in-law ran Hooker & Lent real estate firm.
Bore great-great-grandfather Bunker's Gay name into 1862 San Francisco
was his father-in-law; Founded Shreve & Co.

education.notes: Per Cal Berkeley alumni directory: 'Hooker, Robert Gay, Ph.B. (Mechanics), Real estate, 1117 Pine st, S F'

residences.source: 1880s SF directory (early career, cashier at Am. Sugar Refinery)

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo County CA

birth.historical_notes: DATE DISCREPANCY: Findagrave records 3 Dec 1868. FamilySearch records 31 Dec 1868. Going with Findagrave (cemetery primary source). Father Charles Gay Hooker (H02703) was 48; mother Maria Celia Osgood (X02249) was 32. State: California.

marriages.notes: George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent (1868-1942) — same year birth/death as Bessie. Business partner with Bessie's brother Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) in Hooker & Lent / Lent & Hooker real estate (14 Post Street SF + Mills Building). Bessie's nephew Rodman Lent Hooker (HD3246, Sam Talcott Hooker's grandfather) was named for the Lent partnership.

sources.short: California Academy of Sciences Archives — Alice Eastwood Correspondents

narrative_blocks.header: Married into her brother's business partner
narrative_blocks.body: Bessie Augusta Hooker — daughter of Charles Gay Hooker and Maria Celia Osgood — married George Heydenfeldt Solomon Lent on October 8, 1892 in San Francisco. George (1868-1942) was the business partner of Bessie's brother Robert Gay Hooker in the firm Hooker & Lent (later Lent & Hooker), real estate, with offices at 14 Post Street SF and the Mills Building. The marriage drew the two families' commercial fortunes still closer.

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker line continues to modern Howard cousins
narrative_blocks.body: Through Bessie Augusta and George H. Lent the Hooker line continues into a separate branch of modern California cousins. Their only child Elizabeth Lent (, b.1914) married Dr. Lot D.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote condolence letter to grieving Jane Stanford
narrative_blocks.body: In 1884 — when Bessie Augusta was 16 — Leland Stanford Jr. died of typhoid fever in Florence at age 15, the only son of California's railroad king and former governor Leland Stanford Sr. and his wife Jane. Bessie Augusta Hooker (later Mrs. George H. Lent) wrote a personal condolence letter to Jane Stanford, signing 'Bessie Hooker Lent' from her San Francisco address at 2229 Washington Street: 'Words at such a time, I know, seem cold and meaningless and bring but little comfort, but I felt I could but tell you, after your great kindness to me, how much we sympathize and thought of you in your great sorrow.' The letter is preserved in the Stanford University Archives among the condolence letters re: the death of Leland Stanford Jr. The Hookers and Stanfords moved in overlapping San Francisco mercantile circles; Leland Jr.'s memory two years later (1886) became the foundation of Stanford University.

narrative_blocks.header: Corresponded with botanist Alice Eastwood, 1926
narrative_blocks.body: In 1926 Bessie Hooker Lent appears in the California Academy of Sciences Archives as a correspondent of Alice Eastwood — the legendary botanist and Director of Botany at the Academy from 1894-1949 (with her famous 1906 rescue of 1,500 type specimens during the San Francisco earthquake fire). The single dated entry from that year places Bessie among Eastwood's network of botanical correspondents alongside the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew, the Imperial Botanical Garden in St. Petersburg, and Huntington Gardens. Bessie's interest in botany echoes the Hooker family's long scientific tradition — though no relation, the family shared the surname with Sir William Jackson Hooker (founding Director of Kew) and Sir Joseph Dalton Hooker.

cross_connections.evidence: Bessie's husband George H. Lent partnered with her brother Robert Gay Hooker (H04266) in Hooker & Lent real estate, San Francisco.
cross_connections.display_label: Married her brother's real-estate business partner
was her father-in-law; California '49er pioneer; SF businessman

residences.location: Judicial Township 3, San Mateo County, California, USA

burial.plot_notes: Cypress Lawn Memorial Park, Colma San Mateo Co CA. Cremated.

narrative_blocks.header: Anson died young
narrative_blocks.body: Anson died in 1832 at the age of six. His parents gave the name to a brother born three years later.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.photo_notes: Formal portrait c.1907 (year of death) via Cloudinary

death.address: 870 West Adams Street, Los Angeles CA

sources.short: Willcox Cowboy Hall of Fame (1990 Posthumous Award)
National Cowboy & Western Heritage Museum (1959 Hall of Great Westerners)
Lynn R. Bailey, Henry Clay Hooker and the Sierra Bonita (book)

careers.notes: Hardware store; destroyed by fire Aug 10, 1865
Famous turkey drive 1865; pivoted to beef contracts 1867

careers.employer: U.S. Army (Apache War contracts) and Sierra Bonita Ranch
careers.notes: Founded Sierra Bonita Ranch 1872 in Sulphur Springs Valley AZ. Largest ranch in Arizona at peak (250,000 acres, 30,000 head). Honorary title Colonel.

notable.notable_blurb: Founded Sierra Bonita — Arizona's first cattle ranch

awards.award: National Historic Landmark designation (Sierra Bonita Ranch)
Cowboy Hall of Fame Posthumous Award

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hinsdale brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: Older brother Charles Gay built the San Francisco hardware fortune; younger brother John Daggett funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson — the instrument Hubble used to prove the expanding universe. Henry Clay went south to Arizona Territory and founded its largest cattle empire.

narrative_blocks.header: Sierra Bonita runs in the sixth generation today
narrative_blocks.body: After his 1907 death the ranch passed through son Edwin, grandson Harry, and on to sixth-generation operator Jesse Hooker Davis. The original Herefords from his 1880s importations still graze the 45,000-acre headquarters — Arizona's oldest ranch under continuous single-family ownership.

narrative_blocks.header: Hinsdale farmboy to Arizona cattle king
narrative_blocks.body: Born in 1828 on the family farm settled by his great-grandfather Rev. Bunker Gay, he left Hinsdale at twenty in 1848 and reached Arizona by 1867. He built a 250,000-acre empire two thousand miles from the Connecticut River Valley homestead where his life began.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost everything in a fire, then drove turkeys
narrative_blocks.body: After the 1865 Placerville fire destroyed his hardware store and home, he spent his last thousand dollars on five hundred live turkeys and drove them with two dogs over the Sierra Nevada to the Comstock Lode mining camps. He sold them for $1,750 — the capital that funded his Arizona move.

narrative_blocks.header: A cattle stampede chose Sierra Bonita's site
narrative_blocks.body: While moving ten thousand Texas Longhorns across Arizona in 1872, his herd stampeded and was found grazing in a verdant cienega beneath the Pinaleño Mountains. He homesteaded the spot, named it Sierra Bonita for the range, and built a U-shaped adobe fortress with twenty-inch walls and gun ports.

narrative_blocks.header: Sheltered Wyatt Earp after the Vendetta Ride
narrative_blocks.body: On March 27, 1882, the Earp posse fled to Sierra Bonita pursued by warrants for the killings of the Vendetta Ride. He fed them, gave fresh horses, refused to tell Sheriff Behan which way they had gone, and remained Earp's friend for the rest of his life.

cross_connections.display_label: Nephew Frederick joined him at Angelus-Rosedale, 1933
Nephew Cassius followed his Arizona ranching example

burial.plot_notes: Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA (no CEM entry yet — pending creation)

documents.notes: Standard biography by Lynn R. Bailey.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Founded the ranch in 1872; built and lived in its U-shaped adobe headquarters until his 1907 death.
Born and raised on the Hinsdale homestead until he left for New York at age 20 in 1848.

career.notes: Brief stint in NYC immediately after leaving Hinsdale at age 20.
Attached to the Indian Department, supplying goods on the Missouri frontier.
Mined the El Dorado County diggings before opening a hardware merchant operation at Placerville (Hangtown). His store and home burned in 1865.
Supplied beef to military posts and Indian agencies during the Apache Wars in partnership with Capt. Hugh Hinds.

Founded Arizona's first permanent American cattle ranch. Built U-shaped adobe fortress; expanded to 250,000 acres / 30,000 head. After 1891 drought reorganized as Sierra Bonita Land & Stock Co., capitalized $500,000.

parents.father_research_notes: Peter K. Rockwell of Erie PA (per EH 2705)

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the Buffalo Soldier general

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Born on the homestead; the Gazetteer of Cheshire County (1885) recorded her then residing on the homestead as a widow.
Born on the homestead; the Gazetteer recorded he 'owns and resides on the old homestead' as of 1885.

bio.extra_names: Born John Delos Hooker (per SF directories 1866 and Wikipedia); known professionally as John Daggett Hooker
bio.photo_notes: Photo via Cloudinary; sourced by Sam

death.cause: peritonitis (onset Sunday morning May 21; died Wednesday 8:15 PM May 24, 1911)
death.address: 325 West Adams Street, Los Angeles CA (LA Times obit said "425" — likely typo or renumbering; West Adams Heritage Association + Findagrave + Wikipedia all confirm 325)

sources.short: San Francisco city directories 1866-1885 (Hooker & Co. partner)
Linked institution: California Academy of Sciences (INST093)

notable.notable_blurb: Funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope, Mount Wilson

narrative_blocks.header: From Hinsdale farm to California iron baron
narrative_blocks.body: Educated at Holliston Academy and Williams College, he went to California in 1861 to join brother Charles Gay's San Francisco hardware firm. By December 1865 he had relocated to Los Angeles and built his fortune as Vice President of Baker Iron Works and President of Western Union Oil Company.

narrative_blocks.header: Funded one of history's most consequential telescopes
narrative_blocks.body: His $45,000 pledge in 1906 funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson — total gifts ultimately reached about $100,000. Edwin Hubble used the telescope in the 1920s to prove the universe is expanding, the foundational discovery of modern cosmology.

narrative_blocks.header: Charter member of Southern California Academy
narrative_blocks.body: Per Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy he was a charter member of the Southern California Academy of Sciences, founded 1891 in Los Angeles. He opened his private West Adams observatory to fellow Academy members.

narrative_blocks.header: John Muir wrote his late books here
narrative_blocks.body: Naturalist John Muir returned four times to write in the garret of the Hooker West Adams house in 1910-1911, doing much of his late-life work there. After Hooker's May 1911 death Muir wrote his own daughter Helen: 'I wonder if leaves feel lonely when they see their neighbors falling.'

narrative_blocks.header: His name passed to a great-nephew
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother Charles Gay's grandson — born in 1903 to Robert Gay Hooker and Elizabeth Lawton Shreve — was named John Rodman Hooker in his honor as the great-uncle who had funded the telescope. Tony Hooker, the modern descendant who has assembled much of the pedigree, traces his line through this John Rodman.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers, three California fortunes
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Henry Hooker Sr. of Hinsdale who made California fortunes — Charles Gay built the family hardware firm in San Francisco, Henry Clay founded Arizona's first cattle ranch in 1872, and John Daggett built the LA iron-and-oil empire that funded the telescope. Three brothers, three industries, three transformative Western enterprises.

cross_connections.display_label: Great-nephew was named in his honor after the telescope years.
Hosted Muir for late-life writing in his West Adams garret.
Funded his Mt. Wilson telescope, then estrangement before death.
built blast furnaces and founded the iron town of Anniston

careers.employer: Hooker & Co. (with brother Charles G. Hooker + Isaac H. Cory)
careers.notes: Brought into the family hardware-import firm in San Francisco. Built initial fortune in hardware and iron pipe trade.
Major LA iron and steel-pipe firm. Expanded family fortune significantly after 1886 LA move.
Diversified into California oil after iron-pipe success.

education.notes: Per EH 2709: preparatory education at Holliston Academy MA before Williams College
Per EH 2709: undergraduate at Williams College before going to California in 1861

memberships.notes: Per EH 2709: 'a member from its inception.' Founded 1891 in Los Angeles. NOTE: Wikipedia article on JDH says he 'founded the California Academy of Sciences' (founded 1853 in SF) — that is incorrect per EH primary source. He was a member from inception of the SOUTHERN California Academy of Sciences in LA, distinct from the older SF institution.
Wikipedia attributes 'founding' role; ambiguous given he didn't reach SF until 1861 (CAS founded 1853). May be conflation with Southern California Academy of Sciences.
Per LA Times May 25, 1911 obituary: "active member of the California Club"

Per LA Times May 25, 1911 obituary: "He was a member of the board of directors of the Southern California Academy of Sciences." This confirms the SCAS (LA, founded 1891) — NOT the California Academy of Sciences (SF, founded 1853) which Wikipedia incorrectly cited. Director-level membership suggests substantial active engagement, not just charter membership.

burial.plot_notes: Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA. Family plot with wife Katharine Putnam (X02255), son Laurence Whitney (H04279), and daughter Marian Osgood (H04280).

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Grew up on the Hinsdale homestead before sailing for California; later funded the 100-inch Hooker Telescope at Mount Wilson Observatory.

bio.extra_names: Born May 2, 1849 Milwaukee WI per EH 2709. Some records spell first name 'Katherine' (EH) or 'Katharine' (Wikipedia, West Adams Heritage). Display name uses Wikipedia/modern spelling.

death.address: 224 East Mission Street, Santa Barbara CA

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Osgood Putnam of San Francisco (per EH 2709)
parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Noble Whitney (1822-Jun 23, 1863) — died young at 41 in CA; buried Cypress Lawn Memorial Park Colma SF (per Findagrave). Mother of Katharine. Daughter of Josiah Dwight Whitney Sr (1786-1869, Westfield MA → Northampton MA) and his wife. SISTER of geologist Josiah Dwight Whitney Jr (1819-1896, California State Geologist 1860-1874, Harvard professor, Mt. Whitney named for him) AND of Yale Sanskrit scholar William Dwight Whitney (1827-1894). Both uncles became Katharine's scientific connection on the maternal side. The Whitneys are NOT Hooker descendants — they married INTO the Hooker line via Katharine's marriage to John Daggett Hooker.

sources.short: West Adams Heritage Association — Katharine Putnam Hooker biography
Samuel Marshall Ilsley, 'Katharine Hooker: A Memoir' (1935)
Helen Wright, 'Explorer of the Universe: A Biography of George Ellery Hale' (1966)
Kevin Starr, 'The Dream Endures: California Enters the 1940s' (1997)

notable.notable_blurb: Italian travel writer; West Adams society leader

narrative_blocks.header: Survived two shipwrecks before age fourteen
narrative_blocks.body: In 1853 four-year-old Katharine and her mother were rescued from the wrecked SS Tennessee approaching San Francisco. Nine years later as a fourteen-year-old she escaped the burning Paul Pry steamer as it ran aground on Alcatraz.

narrative_blocks.header: Half Dome by rope, Grand Canyon on foot
narrative_blocks.body: As a teenager she climbed Half Dome in Yosemite by rope and walked to the bottom of the Grand Canyon and back, refusing the easy way down on muleback. At age 85 she took an automobile trip to Vancouver.

narrative_blocks.header: Italian travel writer with John Muir as houseguest
narrative_blocks.body: After her first 1896 trip to Italy she became fluent in Italian and wrote four travel books on the country, illustrated by daughter Marian's photographs. Her West Adams Italian garden became a salon for John Muir, William James, George Ellery Hale, and David Starr Jordan.

narrative_blocks.header: At the heart of George Hale's friendship circle
narrative_blocks.body: Hale formed a deep platonic intellectual friendship with Katharine and her companion Alicia 'Ellie' Mosgrove, finding 'some of his happiest hours' in her Italian garden. Husband John grew jealous and by 1909 had barred Hale from the house in his absence.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived 1906 earthquake while caring for father
narrative_blocks.body: She was visiting her invalid 85-year-old father in San Francisco when the earthquake struck on April 18, 1906. The family evacuated by 3 AM and camped on the sand at Black Point for six days as the city burned.

narrative_blocks.header: Served Progressive Party National Committee for California
narrative_blocks.body: In 1914 she served on the Progressive Party's National Committee representing California, placing her at the political vanguard of the Theodore Roosevelt-era third-party movement. In 1917 she traveled to New York to hear physicist Ernest Rutherford appeal for British WWI aid.

cross_connections.display_label: Received his most expressive late letters after her widowing.
Closest intellectual friend; she was muse to his playful side.
the “Empress of Sixteenth Street” who reshaped a capital district

careers.location: San Francisco / Los Angeles / Santa Barbara CA
careers.notes: Author of four books on Italy: 'Wayfarers in Italy' (1891 private; 1902 Charles Scribner's Sons NY — went through 4 printings by 1905), 'Byways in Southern Tuscany' (1918 Scribner), 'Through the Heel of Italy' (1927 Rae D. Henkle Co. NY), and co-author 'Farmhouses and Small Provincial Buildings in Southern Italy' (1925, with daughter Marian + architect Myron Hunt). All illustrated by Marian's photographs. 'Wayfarers' is still occasionally quoted.
Represented California on the Progressive Party's National Committee in 1914 — politically engaged in the era of Theodore Roosevelt's third-party challenge to the major parties.

burial.plot_notes: Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA — same plot as husband John Daggett Hooker, son Laurence Whitney, daughter Marian Osgood.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale law student; great-nephew of the Whitneys

death.cause: blood poisoning (per obit: pimple on his lip → his face turned black → blood poisoning), age 21

sources.short: West Adams Heritage Association — John Daggett Hooker biography
Tony Hooker pedigree (Laurence Whitney Hooker, b.1873 d.1894)
Yale Law School Class of 1894 obituary record

narrative_blocks.header: Pimple on lip turned to fatal poisoning
narrative_blocks.body: Laurence Whitney Hooker, eldest child of John Daggett Hooker and Katharine Putnam, was a senior in the Class of 1894 at Yale Law School when a small infected pimple on his lip became blood poisoning. Per the obituary, his face turned black as the infection spread. He died on December 11, 1894 in New Haven CT at age 21 — three weeks after his 21st birthday.

narrative_blocks.header: Mountains and Sanskrit in his middle name
narrative_blocks.body: His middle name marked the Whitney brothers, his maternal great-uncles: Josiah, the California geologist Mt. Whitney is named for, and William, the Yale Sanskrit scholar named in his own obituary. Both Whitneys and Laurence all died in 1894 — though his sister Marian later became the first woman to summit Mt. Whitney.

burial.plot_notes: Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA — same plot as parents John Daggett Hooker and Katharine Putnam Hooker (per West Adams Heritage source).

bio.title: Doctor
bio.extra_names: Middle name 'Osgood' likely after her father's brother Charles Osgood Hooker's wife's family / Maria Celia Osgood (Charles Gay's wife)

sources.short: Marian Osgood Hooker Collection, San Diego State University Special Collections
Marian Osgood Hooker Collection, UC Berkeley Environmental Design Archives
Hooker Family Papers, BANC PIC 1977.006-.008, Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley
Watts, Jennifer A. 'Wayfarer In Italy: The Photography of Marian Osgood Hooker.' Southern California Quarterly, Vol. 85 No 1, Spring 2003: 83-100
West Adams Heritage Association — Katharine Putnam Hooker biography

notable.notable_blurb: First woman to summit Mt. Whitney, 1903

narrative_blocks.header: First woman atop Mt Whitney with John Muir
narrative_blocks.body: In 1903 the 27-year-old Marian joined John Muir's small party climbing Mt. Whitney and became the first woman to summit California's highest peak. The mountain had been named for her maternal great-uncle Josiah Dwight Whitney, the California State Geologist.

narrative_blocks.header: Earned medical degree at male-dominated UC Berkeley
narrative_blocks.body: Around age 25 she shifted from artistic interests to scientific study and earned her M.D. at UC Berkeley in 1910 — a decade before women won the federal vote. From 1910 through 1933 she co-authored books on colloid chemistry with biochemist Dr. Martin Fischer at the University of Cincinnati.

narrative_blocks.header: Photographed Italy across five trips abroad
narrative_blocks.body: Between 1896 and 1923 she made five extended trips abroad with her mother and shot rural Italian farmhouses, vernacular buildings, and ancient sites. Her 1925 book 'Farmhouses and Small Provincial Buildings in Southern Italy' influenced Mediterranean Revival architecture in Southern California.

narrative_blocks.header: Family home was a salon for scientists
narrative_blocks.body: After the family moved in 1886 to a mansion at 325 West Adams Street in Los Angeles, the Hooker home became a regular salon for John Muir, William James, George Ellery Hale, and David Starr Jordan. Marian grew up amid this scientific traffic with the Whitney bloodline through her mother and the Hooker scientific patronage through her father.

narrative_blocks.header: Never married, cared for mother until 1935
narrative_blocks.body: After her father's 1911 death she moved with her mother to a Willis Polk-designed San Francisco house, then in 1924 to Santa Barbara, winding down her medical career to be Katharine's primary companion. She died February 14, 1968 at age 92.

cross_connections.display_label: Climbed Mt. Whitney with him in 1903 as the first woman.
Lifelong friend through her mother's intellectual salon.
broke into field science excavating the Amazon

burial.plot_notes: Angelus-Rosedale Cemetery, Los Angeles CA — same plot as parents John Daggett Hooker and Katharine Putnam Hooker, and brother Laurence (per Wikipedia + West Adams Heritage).

careers.notes: Co-authored 'Fats and Fatty Degeneration: A Physico-Chemical Study of Emulsions' (1917), 'Soaps and Proteins: Their Colloid Chemistry in Theory and Practice' (1921), 'The Lyophilic Colloids: Theory and Practice' (1933). Contributed to colloid chemistry research relevant to medical diagnostics and emulsion stability.

Returned to California after Cincinnati research period to take up clinical-academic position. Active until mid-1920s; effectively concluded medical career by late 1920s due to caregiving for aging mother Katharine.

careers.location: Italy, Europe, North Africa, Middle East, California
careers.notes: Author of 'Farmhouses and Small Provincial Buildings in Southern Italy' (1925, with introductions by Katharine Hooker and architect Myron Hunt). Hunt cited the work as inspiration for his Mediterranean-Revival California architecture.

education.notes: Studied art history under school founder Mary Caswell. Graduated 1894. Began photography during school years using Kodak camera; printed her own glass plate negatives. Created 'Marlborough Maidens' photo album (illustrated manuscript with classmates posed as Greek goddesses, etc).
Earned MD in 1910 — entry into a male-dominated profession. Era's medical pathways often did not require a bachelor's degree.

memberships.notes: Joined family pattern of friendship with Sierra Club founder John Muir

burialCemetery.notes: Hinsdale's main 19th-century cemetery, distinct from the older Hooker Cemetery (CEM454) on the Gay-Hooker homestead. The Henry Hooker (H01393) family and the next two generations of his descendants — and several Hinsdale Hookers married into the Pike family — are interred here.
